create_instance.title: New {name} Connection
create_instance.verification_strength_help: Choose Strong only for channels/providers whose identity assertions you trust for stronger continuity and linking decisions.
create_instance.auth_mode_description: Choose how customer browsers bootstrap SDK sessions. Public-key bootstrap is client-only, while hosted exchange adds a customer-backend secret and one-time bootstrap tokens.
create_instance.auth_mode_browser_description: Store the public `pk_*` key, runtime endpoint, and channel selection in client-side configuration.
create_instance.auth_mode_browser_hosted_description: Store the short-lived `bootstrapToken`, runtime endpoint, and project ID in the browser. Do not store the channel server secret here.
create_instance.auth_mode_server_description: No ABL secret is required for anonymous mode. There is nothing extra to provision on the customer server.
create_instance.auth_mode_server_hosted_description: Store the reveal-once `sk_*` channel secret on the customer backend and exchange it for one-time `bootstrapToken` values.
create_instance.auth_mode_security_public: This key is publishable and may ship in browser code.
create_instance.auth_mode_security_identity: It authenticates SDK bootstrap for the app/channel, not the end user.
create_instance.auth_mode_security_public_hosted: The browser should only receive short-lived `bootstrapToken` values. The `sk_*` secret must stay on the customer backend.
create_instance.auth_mode_security_identity_hosted: Hosted exchange verifies the end user through the customer backend before the browser bootstraps Runtime.

call_overview.title: Call Overview
call_overview.turns: {count, plural, =0 {No turns} one {# turn} other {# turns}}
call_overview.info_description: Provides high-level information about the voice call including status, duration, number of conversation turns, and identifiers. This is your quick snapshot of the entire call.
call_overview.info_how_it_works: Aggregates data from session start/end events, turn counts, and call metadata. Duration is calculated from call start to end time.
call_overview.info_example: Example: A 5-minute call with 12 turns (user spoke 6 times, agent responded 6 times) shows 'Connected' status with phone number and unique call ID.

network_quality.title: Network Quality (MOS)
network_quality.description: Mean Opinion Score from network RTCP data
network_quality.info_description: Mean Opinion Score (MOS) measures voice call quality based on network metrics like packet loss, jitter, and latency. Scores range from 1 (poor) to 5 (excellent). Data is collected from Homer QoS integration.
network_quality.info_how_it_works: Uses RTCP (Real-time Transport Control Protocol) statistics from the voice stream. Proxy MOS measures quality at the SIP proxy, Combined MOS includes end-to-end quality.
network_quality.info_example: Example: MOS 4.2 = Good quality, clear audio. MOS 2.8 = Poor quality, choppy audio with noticeable packet loss. MOS below 3.5 typically causes user complaints.

tts_quality.title: TTS Quality
tts_quality.info_description: Measures the quality of synthesized speech generated by the text-to-speech engine. Evaluates naturalness, pronunciation accuracy, and generation latency.
tts_quality.info_how_it_works: Tracks confidence scores from the TTS provider (e.g., ElevenLabs) and measures time from text input to audio ready. High scores indicate natural-sounding speech.
tts_quality.info_example: Example: High confidence (>90%) with 200ms latency = excellent natural speech generated quickly. Low confidence (<70%) may sound robotic or have mispronunciations.

asr_quality.title: ASR Quality
asr_quality.description: Speech recognition accuracy and confidence metrics
asr_quality.info_description: Measures how accurately the speech-to-text system understood what the user said. Higher confidence scores indicate better recognition.
asr_quality.info_how_it_works: We analyze the confidence score from the ASR engine for each turn. Low confidence (<70%) may indicate unclear audio, accents, or background noise.
asr_quality.info_example: Example: A confidence of 95% means the system is very confident it heard correctly. Below 50% often requires user repetition.

asr_cascade.title: ASR Cascade Detection
asr_cascade.description: Detects cascading recognition failures that frustrate users
asr_cascade.info_description: Identifies when multiple speech recognition issues compound to create a poor user experience. This happens when network problems, low confidence, and user repetition occur together.
asr_cascade.info_how_it_works: We analyze 5 signals: network quality, ASR confidence, user repetition patterns, minimal responses, and agent clarification requests. A weighted risk score determines severity.
asr_cascade.info_example: Example: User says 'book hotel' (network drops packets) → ASR hears 'book' with 45% confidence → Agent asks 'what would you like?' → User repeats 'book hotel' = High Risk Cascade

e2e_latency.title: E2E Response Latency
e2e_latency.description: End-to-end response time from user speech end to agent speech start
e2e_latency.info_description: Measures the total time from when the user stops speaking until the agent starts responding. Lower latency creates more natural conversations.
e2e_latency.info_how_it_works: Tracks the full pipeline: VAD detection → ASR processing → LLM generation → TTS synthesis → audio playback start.
e2e_latency.info_example: Example: 800ms is excellent (feels instant), 1500ms is acceptable, 3000ms+ feels sluggish and may cause user impatience.

barge_in.title: Barge-in Events
barge_in.description: User interruptions while agent is speaking
barge_in.info_description: Tracks when users interrupt the agent mid-response. High barge-in rates may indicate the agent is too verbose or not addressing user needs quickly enough.
barge_in.info_how_it_works: Detects voice activity while TTS playback is active. We distinguish between intentional interruptions and false positives from background noise.
barge_in.info_example: Example: Agent starts long explanation → User interrupts 'just give me the price' → Barge-in detected at 2.3s into agent speech.

dtmf_fallback.title: DTMF Fallback
dtmf_fallback.description: Keypad input usage when voice fails
dtmf_fallback.info_description: Tracks when users resort to pressing phone keys (touch-tone) instead of speaking. High fallback rates indicate voice input is unreliable.
dtmf_fallback.info_how_it_works: Monitors the input_type field in turn events. DTMF fallback usually happens after repeated ASR failures or in noisy environments.
dtmf_fallback.info_example: Example: User tries saying account number 3 times → ASR fails → User presses keys '1234' → DTMF fallback detected.

call_activity.title: Call Activity
call_activity.description: Breakdown of call time by activity type
call_activity.info_description: Shows how call time is distributed: active speech (user + agent talking), processing (agent thinking/generating response), and dead air (awkward silence).
call_activity.info_how_it_works: Sums duration from turn events: speech_duration (active), processing_time (agent delay), silence_duration (gaps with no activity).
call_activity.info_example: Example: 5min call = 3min speech + 1min processing + 1min dead air. High dead air (>20%) creates poor user experience.

speech_to_text.title: Speech-to-Text
speech_to_text.info_description: Shows the ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition) engine settings used for this call. Provider (e.g., Deepgram, Whisper), model version, language, and audio encoding details.
speech_to_text.info_how_it_works: Configuration is set at session start based on project settings or channel defaults. Sample rate (typically 8kHz or 16kHz) and encoding (e.g., mulaw, linear16) affect recognition quality.
speech_to_text.info_example: Example: Deepgram Nova-2 model with 16kHz sample rate provides superior accuracy compared to older models at 8kHz. Higher sample rates = better quality but more bandwidth.

text_to_speech.title: Text-to-Speech
text_to_speech.info_description: Shows the TTS engine settings used to generate agent speech. Includes provider (e.g., ElevenLabs, Google TTS), voice selection, language, and audio output settings.
text_to_speech.info_how_it_works: Configuration determines voice characteristics (gender, accent, tone) and audio quality. Number of syntheses shows how many times the TTS engine was called during the conversation.
text_to_speech.info_example: Example: ElevenLabs 'Rachel' voice in English at 24kHz produces natural-sounding female speech. 15 syntheses for a 12-turn conversation indicates some responses were split into multiple audio segments.

call_termination.title: Call Termination
call_termination.info_description: Shows how and why the call ended. Includes SIP status code, termination reason (user hangup, agent completed, error, timeout), and exact end timestamp.
call_termination.info_how_it_works: SIP status codes follow telephony standards: 200 (normal), 486 (busy), 487 (cancelled), 603 (declined). Reason field provides human-readable explanation.
call_termination.info_example: Example: SIP 200 with 'Completed normally' means successful call completion. SIP 487 with 'User cancelled' means user hung up mid-call. SIP 408 with 'Timeout' indicates network issue.
